title: How should AI licensing work for music? The BPI has some ideas... - Music Ally
text:
This may not be the
best
week to be lobbying the British government about AI and creative-industries policy, if we’re honest. Right now, ministers seem entirely distracted by the question of
whether to kick the Prime Minister out or not
.
That’s unfortunate for British labels body the BPI, which this morning published its ‘
Driving UK Growth: The role of licensing music in the age of AI
‘ report.
However, it should not be too downhearted: the report is a solid setting-out of the music industry’s policy asks around AI, and it may have an impact once the dust settles from the current political power-struggle.
The central message of the report will come as no surprise to regular Music Ally readers: it’s asking the government not to throw the music industry under the bus by allowing AI companies to train on copyrighted material without needing to license it.
Instead, the report – commissioned from consultancy firm WPI Economics – makes the case for the government finding “ways to support and accelerate copyright licensing” between rightsholders and AI firms.
Naturally, there are some numbers to back this argument up. The report claims that rightsholders across the creative industries have struck 274 commercial licensing agreements with AI companies as of 2026.
For music specifically, the report lists 26 key deals so far with *deep breath* Suno, Udio, Klay Vision, Stability AI, Nvidia, ElevenLabs, Spotify, Vermillio, Sound Patrol, Songfox, Sureel, Musical AI, Prorata.ai, Soundlabs, Meta, TikTok, BandLab, Google and Splice.
Many of those deals were signed by the three major labels and Merlin, as well as a few by collecting societies. But the BPI surveyed its independent-label members for the report and found that 16% are “exploring licensing partnerships” already, while 77% are “open to licensing their music for ethical use by AI”.
Back to the main lobbying message. 97% of those companies think “the existing copyright framework is fit for licensing AI systems and critical for their business”, which leads to the report putting the boot in to “uncertainty created by discussion of unnecessary changes to copyright law”.
That’s a reference to ongoing speculation that
the UK government might introduce an ‘text-and-data mining’ exception of some kind for AI companies
, enabling them to train their models on copyrighted material without needing permission or licences.
The BPI’s report also criticises “the opacity of AI training data” – the fact that AI companies don’t reveal what they’ve trained their models on already – claiming that this is “a major barrier preventing AI companies coming to the table for licensing discussions”.
All of this leads to the report’s three main asks of government: to keep the existing copyright regime intact; to force AI companies to be transparent about their training inputs; and to apply this to all models that are “deployed” in the UK – even if they were trained in another country with different rules.
The report also reminds the government of the economic value of the UK’s music industry: £8bn in 2024 including including £4.8bn of exports, while supporting 220,000 jobs.
Finally, the study includes some consumer research which, entirely un-surprisingly, suggests that regular Brits agree strongly with the policy goals of the report’s commissioner.
(Okay, this may be over-snarky. 77% of consumers thinking that rightsholders and artists should be paid if their work is used by AI to create a new song, and the same percentage think artists’ music or vocals shouldn’t be used by an AI company without permission. That
does
ring true as a gauge of the public’s attitudes to GenAI in our opinion.)
“This research demonstrates that there is a viable path to making the UK a global hub for ethical AI-music licensing. Music labels are pioneering licensing deals with ethical AI companies with multiple deals already struck and many more conversations underway,” said the BPI’s chief strategy officer Sophie Jones.
“For this market to scale successfully, the Government must support it through a firm commitment to upholding existing copyright laws and mandating record keeping and disclosure.”
As we said, the report is a clear setting-out of the music industry’s views on AI and licensing, and what it wants the government to do.
Alternative opinions are available, of course: the AI/tech industry has been making its own arguments to the UK government – for example tech trade body TechUK has been
lobbying strongly for a text-and-data mining exception for AI
.
The BPI will be hoping that when the government has finished its current bout of infighting, the relevant ministers will read the report and take its opinions on board when the time comes to finalise any AI legislation.
That said, as
this week’s intense disappointment about the delay of the UK’s planned ban on ticket-touting
shows, even when politicians promise the music business they’ll take a certain path, it doesn’t always turn into legislation as quickly as the industry would like.
Stuart Dredge
Music Ally's Head of Insight
More by Stuart Dredge
